coredata.dc:title: The characterization and performance of a core–shell structured nanoplatform for fluorescence turn-on sensing and selective removal of perfluorooctane substance
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Gong, Zhihai
Li, Ji
Wu, Fuzhong
coredata.dc:description: The high dissociation energy of C-F bonds makes the natural degradation and decomposition of PFAS (per- and poly-fluoroalkyl substances) nearly impossible, leaving the PFAS wastes widely dispersed and permanently persisting in the natural environment. Consequently, detection and recognition techniques, as well as treatment technology, are highly desired for perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) pollutants. In this work, we designed a core–shell magnetic-porous composite structure (denoted as Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY) for the sensing, adsorption, and removal of PFOS, using EY:CTAB (0.5 μM:80 μM, EY = Eosin Y, CTAB = cetyltrimethylammonium bromide) as the sensing probe, Fe3O4 nanoparticles as the core, and porous silica MCM-41 as the shell. The composite structure of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY was confirmed by means of SEM (scanning electron microscope), magnetism, XRD (X-ray diffraction), N2 adsorption/desorption, and TGA (thermal gravimetric analysis). A probe loading level of 28.5 % was determined. The probe showed an emission turn-on effect toward PFOS owing to the viscosity variation of the micelles caused by PFOS. A linear fitting equation was obtained as I/I0 = 1.081 + 0.144 × 106[PFOS], R2 = 0.990, with good sensing selectivity and LOD (limit of detection) of 0.27 μM. In addition, Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY removed PFOS efficiently with an adsorption capacity of 0.126 mg/g. The novelty of this work was the simultaneous sensing, adsorption, and removal of PFOS using a core–shell magnetic-porous composite structure.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PFOS
Optical sensing
Adsorption and removal
Core–shell

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY: Loading guest molecules into Fe3O4@MCM-41
How to sense and adsorb PFOS with Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY
Molecular geometry and theoretical simulation of EY
Photophysical responses of EY towards PFOS
Magnetic core determined by SEM, magnetism, and powder XRD
Porous structure determined by SAXRD, N2 adsorption/desorption, and TGA
Sensing and adsorption property of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY
Emission spectra, working calibration, and selectivity

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The simulated structures of closed-ring form (a), open-ring form (b), and deprotonated open-ring form (c) of EY. The crystal structures of EY in closed-ring form (d), open-ring form (e), and deprotonated open-ring form (f). See Supporting Information for detailed parameters.

The frontier molecular orbitals of EY in closed-ring form (a, HOMO; b, LUMO), open-ring form (c, HOMO; d, LUMO), and deprotonated open-ring form (e, HOMO; f, LUMO). The simulated absorption spectra of these three forms (g).

The absorption (a) and emission (b) spectra of EY (0.5 μM), EY:CTAB (0.5 μM:80 μM), and EY:CTAB:PFOS (0.5 μM:80 μM:12 μM). Inset: the emission quantum yield (Φ) variation of EY against [CTAB] upon various PFOS concentrations.
The SEM images of as-synthesized Fe3O4 sample (a), Fe3O4@SiO2 (b), Fe3O4@MCM-41 (c), and Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY (d). See Supporting Information for more SEM and TEM images.

The magnetic hysteresis loops (a) and powder XRD curves (b) of the as-synthesized Fe3O4 sample, Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@MCM-41, and Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY. The N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and TGA curves of Fe3O4@MCM-41 (c) and Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY (d). Inset a: photos of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY dispersed in Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH = 8.55) without (left) and with (right) a magnet. Inset b: SAXRD curves of Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@MCM-41, and Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY. Inset d: DTG curves of Fe3O4@MCM-41 and Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY.
The emission spectra of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY with increasing PFOS concentrations from 0 to 12 μM. Inset: the I/I0 variation against [PFOS].

The emission spectra of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY upon various interferents (10 μM), including 0, blank; 1, POFS; 2, PFOA; 3, PFBA; 4, PFPA; 5, NFVA; 6, Na+; 7, K+; 8, Mg2+; 9, Ca2+; 10, Fe3+; 11, Zn2+; 12, Cl-; 13, Br-; and 14 CO3 2–. Inset up: I/I0 values with these interferents. Inset down: the emission intensity monitoring of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY at 576 nm upon the addition of PFOS.
The construction strategy and the working mechanism of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY.
Key geometric parameters of the simulated structures and the experimental values of EY. See Supporting Information for more details.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a, from the benzoic acid ring; b, from the xanthene ring.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The photophysical parameters of EY (0.5 μM) upon various concentrations of CTAB and PFOS.
Sensing and adsorption result of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a, recovery = the result obtained from the Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY/that from the HPLC, presented in the unit of %; b, determined by HPLC, presented in the unit of μM, then the relative adsorption ratio was calculated by (original concentration-residual concentration)/ original concentration, presented in the unit of %, and finally the absolute adsorption content was calculated by (original concentration-residual concentration) × 1 mL/0.1 mg, presented in the unit of mmol/g.

head.ce:title: The characterization and performance of a core–shell structured nanoplatform for fluorescence turn-on sensing and selective removal of perfluorooctane substance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of resources and environmental engineer, Guizhou University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of resources and environmental engineer

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of resources and environmental engineer, Guizhou University, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of biological and environmental engineer, Guiyang University, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of biological and environmental engineer

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of biological and environmental engineer, Guiyang University, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The high dissociation energy of C-F bonds makes the natural degradation and decomposition of PFAS (per- and poly-fluoroalkyl substances) nearly impossible, leaving the PFAS wastes widely dispersed and permanently persisting in the natural environment. Consequently, detection and recognition techniques, as well as treatment technology, are highly desired for perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) pollutants. In this work, we designed a core–shell magnetic-porous composite structure (denoted as Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY) for the sensing, adsorption, and removal of PFOS, using EY:CTAB (0.5 μM:80 μM, EY = Eosin Y, CTAB = cetyltrimethylammonium bromide) as the sensing probe, Fe3O4 nanoparticles as the core, and porous silica MCM-41 as the shell. The composite structure of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY was confirmed by means of SEM (scanning electron microscope), magnetism, XRD (X-ray diffraction), N2 adsorption/desorption, and TGA (thermal gravimetric analysis). A probe loading level of 28.5 % was determined. The probe showed an emission turn-on effect toward PFOS owing to the viscosity variation of the micelles caused by PFOS. A linear fitting equation was obtained as I/I0 = 1.081 + 0.144 × 106[PFOS], R2 = 0.990, with good sensing selectivity and LOD (limit of detection) of 0.27 μM. In addition, Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY removed PFOS efficiently with an adsorption capacity of 0.126 mg/g. The novelty of this work was the simultaneous sensing, adsorption, and removal of PFOS using a core–shell magnetic-porous composite structure.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The continuous development of modern society is now facing an increasing number of contaminants, also known as emerging contaminants (ECs), such as the waste from industrial manufacturing and modern packaging. These ECs vary in form and chemical composition, but they are widely distributed and nearly impossible to degrade naturally, causing long-term and persistent harm to mammals and thus making them critical issues in environmental protection [1,2]. As a representative class of EC, per- and poly-fluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), whose carbon chains are partially or fully fluorinated, are frequently mentioned due to their extensive use in modern life, including non-stick coating films for kitchenware, adhesives, food packaging, and fire-extinguishing fluids [3,4]. The high dissociation energy of C-F bonds makes the natural degradation and decomposition of these PFAS nearly impossible, leaving PFAS waste widely dispersed and permanently persisting in the natural environment. Through bioaccumulation in food chains, the hazardous effects eventually reach and harm the top of the food chain--humans. For instance, in 2001, perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS) was designated as a persistent organic pollutant by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA). The harmful effects of PFOS on the liver, bladder, growth, immune system, and nervous system have been well reported and discussed [5–8]. Consequently, detection and recognition techniques, as well as treatment technologies, are highly needed for PFOS pollutants.

It is easy to satisfy the requirement of detecting and quantifying PFOS pollutants through modern analytical techniques, such as mass spectrometry, capillary zone electrophoresis, electrochemical analysis, photochemical reaction, and fluorescence sensing [9–12]. Despite the precise results of the above-mentioned techniques, they face drawbacks and disadvantages, including limited electrochemical activity, unsatisfactory sensitivity, and background noise [13]. Most of these analytical techniques are effective at detecting PFOS substances but incapable of adsorbing or decomposing them. As a consequence, some innovative designs and strategies should be implemented to achieve the simultaneous detection and removal of PFOS pollutants.

The literature has suggested the idea of a nanocomposite structure composed of a fluorescence sensing component and a magnetic adsorption component [14]. This composite structure combines the features of magnetic adsorption/removal and fluorescence sensing, thus satisfying the above-mentioned requirement for the simultaneous detection and removal of PFOS pollutants. There are examples of fluorescent probes for sensing PFOS pollutants, including both emission turn-off and turn-on types. For example, Tan’s research group has reported versatile probes for the optical sensing of PFOS substances, including both emission turn-on and turn-off ones, such as carbon-dots:berberine, Eosin Y:Polyethyleneimine, and Erythrosin B:Hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide [15–17]. In these reports, the probe transfers its energy to the auxiliary/co-existing reagent, showing a weak emission. Then the PFOS interacts with the auxiliary/co-existing reagent and releases the probe, showing an emission turn-on effect. Emission turn-off sensing systems were also reported and discussed by Tan and Wang using quantum CdS and carbon dots probes [12,18,19]. The above-mentioned literature inspired new pathways for the optical detection of PFOS. Regarding the magnetic adsorption component, the literature has suggested an effective composite structure consisting of a magnetic-guiding core and an amorphous silica shell [20,21]. The outer shell has a porous structure and effectively adsorbs guest molecules, such as the probe and PFOS, while the core is responsible for magnetic removal after adsorbing target molecules.

Inspired by the above ideas and literature, in this work, we designed a core–shell composite structure (denoted as Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY) for the sensing, adsorption, and removal of PFOS, using Eosin Y:CTAB as the sensing probe, Fe3O4 nanoparticles as the core, and porous silica MCM-41 as the shell. The CTAB here served as the auxiliary reagent of Eosin Y (EY). This work selected MCM-41 as the supporting host due to its highly ordered hexagonal tunnels, which have been proven effective in adsorbing guest molecules. More importantly, its synthesis required CTAB as a template reagent, which enabled effective adsorption of the sensing probe (Eosin Y:CTAB). Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY was characterized by means of morphology comparison, magnetic analysis, porous dynamics, and probe loading content. Upon PFOS addition, an emission turn-on phenomenon was expected from Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY. The sensing and adsorption performance of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY was recorded and discussed. The novelty of this work is the simultaneous sensing, adsorption, and removal of PFOS using a core–shell magnetic-porous composite structure, which will be demonstrated below in detail.

The construction strategy and working mechanism of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY can be found in Scheme 1 . All synthesis-related reagents were of analytical grade and used as received, including Eosin Y, sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), FeCl3·6H2O, glycol, AcONa, NH3·H2O, TEOS (tetraethyl orthosilicate), cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), perfluorooctane sulfonate (PFOS), perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), heptafluorobutyric acid (PFBA), pentafluoropropionic acid (PFPA), and nonafluorovaleric acid (NFVA). The magnetic property was recorded using an MPM5-XL-5 superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) magnetometer. The sample micromorphology was analyzed on a Hitachi S-4800 microscope. The powder XRD (PXRD) and small-angle XRD (SAXRD) curves were measured using a Rigaku D/Max-Ra X-ray diffractometer (λ = 0.15418 nm) and a Rigaku-Dmax 2500 diffractometer (λ = 0.154 nm, scanning step = 0.02°), respectively. The sample porous structure was analyzed using a Nova l000 analyzer with the BET (Brunauer-Emmett-Teller) model. Thermal dynamics were performed using a Perkin-Elmer thermal analyzer. The absorption spectra were recorded using a Varian INOVA 300 spectrometer. The emission spectra and quantum yields were determined using a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a reference method [22]. Using the single crystal structure as the initial geometry, theoretical calculations were performed at the RB3LYP/6-31G(d) level in vacuum using Orca (version 5.04) and Firefly (version 8.2). The simulated absorption spectra were plotted using Multiwfn (version 3.8, broadening function = Gaussian, FWHM = 0.66667 eV), and the frontier molecular orbitals were plotted using wxMacMolPlt (version 7.4.4, contour level = 0.05).

The core–shell structure of Fe3O4@MCM-41 was synthesized according to a literature-reported three-step procedure, as described below [20]. First, the magnetic particles were prepared. To a mixture of SDS (2.0 g), FeCl3·6H2O (5.4 g), and AcONa (14.4 g), 100 mL of glycol was added and stirred at room temperature for 30 min. The resulting solution was transferred into an autoclave (capacity = 50 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 12 h. The resulting mixture was naturally cooled and filtered to yield a dark brown powder, which was washed with deionized water and dried at 50 °C before being used for the next step (Yield: 77 %). Then, the fresh Fe3O4 particles (0.3 g) were mixed with ethanol (40 mL) and treated in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min. During this time, the following reagents were slowly added, including deionized water (20 mL), NH3·H2O (1 ml), and TEOS (0.2 g). The resulting suspension was soaked in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min and then stirred at room temperature for 5 h. The solid product was filtered off, collected, washed with deionized water, and dried at 50 °C before being used for the next step (Yield: 0.26 g). Finally, to a mixture of ethanol (100 mL) and deionized water (100 mL), the following reagents were added, including CTAB (0.3 g), NH3·H2O (2 mL), TEOS (0.8 g), and 0.2 g of Fe3O4@SiO2. The obtained mixture was allowed to react at room temperature for 6 h. Then, the solid product was soaked into a mixture of ethanol (100 mL) and HCl (5 mL) to extract the template reagent CTAB. After being reflexed at 85 °C for 36 h, the solid residue was filtered off, flushed with deionized water, and obtained as Fe3O4@MCM-41 (Yield: 0.39 g). Before loading guest molecules, Fe3O4@MCM-41 was heated and activated at 50 °C under vacuum overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY: Loading guest molecules into Fe3O4@MCM-41
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing probe Eosin Y (EY) and its auxiliary reagent CTAB were first mixed with a molar ratio of 1:160. 0.2 g of the guest molecules (EY:CTAB = 1:160) were dissolved in ethanol (10 mL) and mixed with the above-synthesized Fe3O4@MCM-41 (0.1 g). The final mixture was stirred at room temperature for 5 h to perform a round of the loading procedure. Four more rounds were performed under the same conditions to achieve loading equilibrium. The solid product was finally collected, washed with deionized water and ethanol, and dried naturally to yield Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: How to sense and adsorb PFOS with Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY
ce:section.ce:para: First, a stock suspension of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY (1 mg/mL) was prepared using Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH = 8.55) as the solvent. The PFOS standard solution was mixed with Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY suspension (0.1 mL) and then diluted to 1 mL with deionized water. After being shaken for 10 min, its spectra were recorded. For the adsorption operation, the concentration of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY was kept at 1 mg/mL. Then, a controlled amount of PFOS was added, and the resulting mixture was shaken for 10 min. After magnetically removing Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY, the PFOS residual concentration in the transparent solution was determined by HPLC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular geometry and theoretical simulation of EY
ce:section.ce:para: The dyes Eosin Y, erythrosine B, and rhodamine all share similar molecular structures, typically composed of a substituted xanthene ring and a benzoic acid ring. Literature has reported structural transformations in these rhodamine-like molecules, which exist in two representative configurations, the open-ring form and the closed-ring form [23]. Under suitable conditions, such as the presence of excess protons, metal cations, or small organic molecules, a structural transformation between these two forms can occur. Since these two configurations exhibit distinct photophysical properties, the variations in their corresponding optical signals can be modulated as a sensing output, thereby enabling these rhodamine-like molecules to function as optical probes [23,24]. Using the structures of rhodamine-like molecules as a reference, it is reasonable to hypothesize that EY may adopt three representative structural configurations: (1) the closed-ring form, (2) the open-ring form, and (3) the deprotonated open-ring form. Their simulated molecular structures, along with those obtained from single-crystal analysis, are shown in Fig. 1 . Key structural parameters are compared and listed in Table 1 . The three simulated geometries of EY closely resemble the single-crystal geometries, with bond lengths in the simulated geometries generally being longer than those in the single crystals, which is a widely reported phenomenon [25–27]. The frontier molecular orbitals of the three forms of EY are presented in Fig. 2 . Clearly, the HOMO (highest occupied molecular orbital) of the closed-ring form is primarily localized on the Br-substituted xanthene ring, while the LUMO (lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) is mainly distributed on the benzoic acid ring. The open-ring form of EY exhibits a similar orbital distribution to the closed-ring form. In contrast, for the deprotonated open-ring form, both the HOMO and LUMO are localized on the Br-substituted xanthene ring. The first 70 electronic transitions of the three simulated geometries of EY were calculated, and the corresponding simulated absorption spectra are shown in Fig. 2. For the closed-ring form, all electronic transitions occur in the ultraviolet region, with wavelengths shorter than 330 nm, indicating no significant absorption in the visible region (350 nm-700 nm). In contrast, the two open-ring forms, in addition to the high-energy electronic transitions in the ultraviolet region which are similar to those of the closed-ring form, exhibit additional low-energy electronic transitions in the visible region, with absorption peaks at ∼ 463 nm for the open-ring form and 437 nm for the deprotonated open-ring form, respectively. These distinct optical properties suggests that EY could serve as a potential optical probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photophysical responses of EY towards PFOS
ce:section.ce:para: According to literature reports, like other rhodamine-derived molecules, the photophysical properties of EY, including its emission wavelength and quantum yield, are sensitive to pH variations [17]. The theoretical calculations and analysis on the three structural configurations of EY, as discussed earlier, support this conclusion. It has been reported that an acidic environment tends to quench EY emission, whereas an alkaline environment leads to a red shift in the emission wavelength and an enhancement of EY emission. To eliminate pH-induced effects on the EY probe, this study utilized Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer (pH = 8.55), as mentioned in Section 2.4. The photophysical responses of EY to CTAB and PFOS were investigated through absorption (abs) and emission (em) spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 , pure EY (no CTAB, no PFOS) exhibits two strong absorption bands, peaking at 231 nm and 502 nm. This absorption spectrum closely resembles the simulated absorption spectrum of the open-ring form of EY in Fig. 2. Consequently, the low-energy absorption band at 502 nm is attributed to the onset singlet electronic transition between the HOMO and LUMO. The emission spectrum of pure EY (no CTAB, no PFOS) features a yellow fluorescence peaking at 560 nm, with a quantum yield (Φ) of 0.17. In the presence of CTAB (80 μM), the onset electronic transition band undergoes a red shift to 533 nm. Correspondingly, the emission peak red-shifts to 570 nm, and the emission quantum yield decreases to 0.08. However, the band shape of both the onset electronic transition and emission spectra remains unchanged. This phenomenon, including the red-shifted absorption/emission peaks and reduced emission quantum yields, has been explained by the formation of “pre-micellar aggregates” or “dye rich-micelles” between EY and CTAB molecules through electrostatic attraction [17]. When both CTAB (80 μM) and PFOS (12 μM) are present, the onset electronic transition of EY is further red-shifted to 535 nm, and its absorption intensity increases compared to that (533 nm) in the presence of CTAB (80 μM) alone. Similarly, the emission peak red-shifts slightly to 575 nm, and the emission quantum yield significantly increases to 0.39, compared to those (570 nm and 0.08) in the presence of only CTAB (80 μM). This results in an emission turn-on effect for EY:CTAB (0.5 μM:80 μM), triggered by the presence of PFOS. Literature has attributed this effect to changes in micelle microviscosity upon the addition of PFOS [28,29].

By fixing the EY concentration at 0.5 μM, the photophysical parameters of EY, including absorption peak, emission peak, and emission quantum yield, were systematically measured under varying concentrations of CTAB and PFOS. These results, summarized in Table 2 , allow for the determination of the optimal molar ratio between EY, CTAB, and PFOS. In the absence of CTAB, the addition of PFOS (12 μM) caused a minor red shift in both the absorption and emission peaks of EY (absorption: 502 nm vs 504 nm, emission: 560 nm vs. 562 nm). Meanwhile, the emission quantum yield decreased from 0.17 ([PFOS] = 0 μM) to 0.13 ([PFOS] = 12 μM). This result suggests a quenching effect of PFOS on EY, likely due to direct interactions between PFOS and EY in the absence of CTAB. In the absence of PFOS, the addition of CTAB resulted in both absorption and emission red shifts. The emission quantum yield of EY initially decreased from 0.17 ([CTAB] = 0 μM) to 0.04 ([CTAB] = 10 μM) and then partially recovered to 0.09 ([CTAB] = 100 μM). When both CTAB and PFOS were present, the emission quantum yield of EY:CTAB increased with increasing PFOS concentration. As shown in Table 2, the optimal molar ratio was determined to be [EY]:[CTAB] = 0.5 μM:80 μM, which exhibited the lowest Φ value of 0.08 at [PFOS] = 0 μM and the highest Φ value of 0.39 at [PFOS] = 12 μM. This molar ratio was selected for subsequent sensing experiments.

The design strategy of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY is briefly illustrated in Scheme 1 and explained as follows. Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY is constructed based on a core–shell structure, where each component plays a distinct functional role. The Fe3O4 core serves as a magnetic carrier, enabling site-specific aggregation and facilitating easy removal after adsorbing PFOS molecules. To prevent Fe(III)/Fe(II) ions from quenching the fluorescence of EY, a thin layer of amorphous SiO2 is coated onto the Fe3O4 core. This SiO2 layer also provides a suitable surface for the subsequent growth of the MCM-41 molecular sieve. This work chose MCM-41 as the supporting host due to its highly ordered porous structure [21]. The synthesis of MCM-41 requires CTAB as a template reagent. As a result, the MCM-41 pores are expected to have sufficient space to accommodate the guest molecules of EY:CTAB, which further enables the adsorption of PFOS molecules. Initially, the EY emission is quenched by the auxiliary reagent of CTAB, resulting in a weak emission signal. Upon addition of PFOS, the MCM-41 tunnels first aggregate and adsorb the PFOS molecules. The interaction between PFOS and CTAB alters the micelle microviscosity, leading to a restoration of EY fluorescence and thus an emission turn-on effect that enables the detection of PFOS [30,31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Magnetic core determined by SEM, magnetism, and powder XRD
ce:section.ce:para: The micromorphology of the as-synthesized Fe3O4, Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@MCM-41, and Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY was analyzed using their SEM (scanning electron microscopy) images. As shown in Fig. 4 , the Fe3O4 nanoparticles exhibit a nearly spherical morphology with an average diameter of ∼ 100 nm. Their surface appears rough with noticeable bumps and grooves, likely due to cracks and defects formed during the nanocrystal growth. Upon coating with a thin layer of amorphous SiO2, the Fe3O4@SiO2 particles exhibit a smoothers surface, with their diameter increasing to ∼ 120 nm. Further modification with MCM-41 results in an increased diameter of ∼ 300 nm, with the surface becoming even smoother. The core–shell layered structure and ordered structure on the particle edge can be seen from their TEM images shown in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information). After loading the guest molecules (EY:CTAB), the morphology of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY (see Fig. S2 of Supporting Information for an overall view) remains nearly identical to that of Fe3O4@MCM-41, indicating that the loading process minimally affects the particle morphology. The elemental mapping shown in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information) confirms the Fe3O4-cored structure, with the guest molecules (EY:CTAB) loaded into the tunnels of the outer shell. The magnetic properties of the as-synthesized Fe3O4 sample, Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@MCM-41, and Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY were investigated using magnetic hysteresis loops measurements, as shown in Fig. 5 . The as-synthesized Fe3O4 exhibits the highest saturation magnetization value of 85.1 emu⋅g−1. With the subsequent coating and embedding steps, the relative weight fraction of Fe3O4 decreases, leading to a gradual reduction in saturation magnetization: 70.6 emu⋅g−1 for Fe3O4@SiO2, 53.6 emu⋅g−1 Fe3O4@MCM-41, and 45.9 emu⋅g−1 Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY, respectively. Importantly, no significant hysteresis is observed, confirming that all samples retain their superparamagnetic nature. This property ensures good dispersion in solution under the absence of an external magnetic field, while, allowing for magnetic-guided separation upon exposure to a magnet, as shown in Fig. 5. The crystalline structure of the as-synthesized Fe3O4 was characterized using powder XRD (PXRD), with the corresponding patterns displayed in Fig. 5. The Fe3O4 sample exhibits several sharp and well-resolved diffraction peaks at 2θ = 30.1°, 35.4°, 43.3°, 57.2°, and 62.6°, which match the characteristic reflections reported for Fe3O4 nanoparticles in the literature [20]. This result confirms the successful synthesis of the Fe3O4 core. These diffractions peaks remain well preserved in Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@MCM-41, and Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY, confirming that despite the sequential SiO2 and MCM-41 growth and EY:CTAB embedding, the Fe3O4 core structure remains intact.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Porous structure determined by SAXRD, N2 adsorption/desorption, and TGA
ce:section.ce:para: The small-angle XRD (SAXRD) patterns of Fe3O4@SiO2, Fe3O4@MCM-41, and Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY were recorded and compared in Fig. 5 to evaluate their porous microstructure. Fe3O4@SiO2 shows no distinct peaks, indicating the absence of long-range ordered structure. For Fe3O4@MCM-41, a well-defined (100) diffraction peak is observed at 2.4° (2θ), along with two weaker peaks corresponding to (110) at 4.3° and (200) at 5.1°. These peaks align well with literature reports on MCM-41 samples, confirming the successful formation of highly ordered mesoporous channels [21]. A similar diffraction pattern is observed for Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY, with peaks slightly shift to 2.5° for (100), 4.4° for (110) and 5.2° for (200), respectively. This confirms that the regular mesoporous structure of MCM-41 is well preserved after the incorporation of the guest molecules (EY:CTAB). To further analyze the porous microstructure, N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms were recorded for Fe3O4@MCM-41 and Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY, as shown in Fig. 5. Both samples exhibit Type-IV isotherms, which are consistent with previously reported MCM-41 samples [21]. The adsorption volume of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY (82.4 cm3/g) is significantly lower than that of Fe3O4@MCM-41 (321.3 cm3/g), suggesting two possible explanations. One reason is that the guest molecules (EY:CTAB) are embedded inside the internal pores of the MCM-41 layer, reducing the available adsorption pore volume. The other reason is that the outer surface of the MCM-41 layer is partially covered by the guest molecules (EY:CTAB), hindering N2 adsorption/desorption and hereby reducing the apparent adsorption volume. To determine the dominant factor for this reduction, TGA (thermal gravimetric analysis) was conducted on Fe3O4@MCM-41 and Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY, and the results are presented in Fig. 5. The Fe3O4@MCM-41 sample exhibits a weight loss of 3.0 % (from 100 % to 97.0 %) between 40 °C and 110 °C, which is attributed to the evaporation of adsorbed water molecules. A gradual weight loss occurs between 110 °C and 598 °C, decreasing the total weight from 97.0 % (at 110 °C) to 91.5 % (at 598 °C), likely due to silica dehydration and the release of residual adsorbed molecules. In contrast, Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY exhibits a slightly higher initial weight loss of 5.2 % (from 100 % to 94.8 %) between 40 °C and 110 °C, again due to water evaporation. However, a sharp weight loss from 94.8 % at 110 °C to 66.3 % at 598 °C is observed, with a distinct endothermic peak of 410 °C. This weight loss corresponds to the thermal decomposition and evaporation of the guest molecules (EY:CTAB). The loading value of the EY:CTAB is determined by the difference of weight loss% at 598 °C and calculated to be 25.2 wt% (=91.5 % − 66.3 %). Given this high loading content, it is inferred that the guest molecules (EY:CTAB) primarily reside within in the internal pores of the MCM-41 layer rather than being simply adsorbed onto the outer surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing and adsorption property of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY
Emission spectra, working calibration, and selectivity
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 6 presents the emission spectra of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY upon increasing PFOS concentrations from 0 to 12 μM. In the absence of PFOS, a relatively weak emission is observed at 571 nm. As the PFOS concentration increases, the emission intensity of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY gradually enhances. At PFOS concentration of 12 μM, the emission intensity at 576 nm is increased minorly, indicating that the sensing process reaches saturation. It is still observed that the emission wavelength is red-shifted (by 5 nm) as the PFOS concentration increases, suggesting an increasing interaction between PFOS and the guest molecules (EY:CTAB). To quantify this emission enhancement, the data were analyzed using the Stern-Volmer equation, as described in Equation 1. Here I represents the emission intensity, I0 is the emission intensity in the absence of PFOS, KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant, and [PFOS] is the PFOS concentration.
I/I0 = 1 + KSV[PFOS](1)

The plot of I/I0 against [PFOS] follows a linear trend, yielding the fitted equation of I/I0 = 1.081 + 0.144 × 106[PFOS], R2 = 0.990. From this calibration curve, the LOD (limit of detection) value is determined as 0.27 μM. Based on this working equation, the practical sensing performance of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY is evaluated. As shown in Table 3 , the optical sensing method exhibits a systematic negative bias, with recovery values ranging from 89.4 % to 96.2 % within the 1.0–10.0 μM PFOS concentration range. Notably, at lower PFOS concentrations (< 5 μM), the sensing errors (recovery < 91 %) appear more significant, compared to those (>96 %) at higher concentrations (5.0–10.0 μM). This discrepancy likely arises because, at low PFOS concentrations (< 5 μM), some PFOS molecular may become adsorbed or trapped within the MCM-41 microstructure, effectively reducing the detectable PFOS concentration and introducing a negative error. When the PFOS concentration exceeds the effective sensing range (e.g., 15 μM), a further diminished recovery of 80.8 % is observed, likely due to sensor saturation.

Sensing selectivity, defined as the ability of a probe to exhibit a unique response toward a target analyte in a complex environment containing competing species, is a critical parameter for evaluating the performance of an optical sensing system. The selectivity of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY was assessed by monitoring its emission spectra in the presence of various competing species and common interferents, including perfluorooctanoic acid (PFOA), heptafluorobutyric acid (PFBA), pentafluoropropionic acid (PFPA), nonafluorovaleric acid (NFVA), Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Cl-, Br-, and CO3 2–. As shown in Fig. 7 , Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY exhibits excellent selectivity, with only one notable exception: PFOA, which significantly interferes with the sensing response (I/I0 = 2.3, compared to 2.61 for PFOS). This interference is likely due to the structural similarity between PFOA and PFOS, particularly their fluorocarbon chains, which can insert into the CTAB micelles. This interaction alters the micellar viscosity, thereby inducing an emission variation in EY:CTAB [17]. Since PFOA and PFOS share identical fluorocarbon backbones, the observed interference from PFOA is unsurprising. In contrast, other fluoroalkyl species, PFBA, PFPA, and NFVA, reduce the emission intensity of the EY:CTAB rather than enhancing it. This suggests that these species destabilize the CTAB micelles, leading to emission quenching. A similar quenching effect is observed for the inorganic ions, accompanied by an emission redshift, which is likely due to the direct interactions between these ions and the EY:CTAB.

The sensing behavior of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY is driven by emission variation induced by PFOS, which involves a series of dynamics processes, including the interactions between CTAB chains and PFOS and the resulting changes in micelles viscosity. Therefore, evaluating the response time is essential, as it provides insight into the adsorption and capture dynamics of PFOS molecules by Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY. The response time was determined by monitoring the emission intensity at 576 nm following the addition of a drop of PFOS into Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY. As shown in Fig. 7, the emission intensity initially decreases within the first 20 s, likely dye to the adsorption of PFOS into the MCM-41 layer of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY. Subsequently, the emission intensity gradually increases over 20–925 s, during which time the adsorbed PFOS molecules are accumulated and begin to affect the viscosity of the micelles and increase their emission intensity. After 930 s, the emission intensity stabilizes, suggesting that adsorption equilibrium has been reached. This response time is significantly longer than previously reported values for MCM-41-based optical sensing platforms (<20 s) [21,32]. The discrepancy arises because the sensing mechanism of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY involves multiple time-consuming steps in solution, including PFOS adsorption, interaction between the chains of CTAB and PFOS, and corresponding structural reorganization. In contrast, literature reports primarily describe gas-phase sensing, where the high diffusion and transport rates of gaseous analytes enable much faster response times. Thus, the extended response time of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY is reasonable given the inherent differences between solution-phase and gas-phase sensing dynamics.

The adsorption and removal efficiency of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY was evaluated by monitoring the residual PFOS concentration after five consecutive treatment cycles. As shown in Table 3, a significant reduction in PFOS concentration was observed after treatment with Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY, directly confirming its ability to adsorb and remove PFOS from solution. The relative adsorption ratios (as defined in Table 3) ranged from 40.0 % to 88.1 %, with a general trend of higher adsorption efficiency at elevated PFOS concentrations. Conversely, at lower PFOS concentrations (< 0.1 μM), the relative adsorption ratio decreased. This behavior can be attributed to concentration-driven adsorption, where higher PFOS concentrations promote faster and more efficient adsorption onto Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY. At an initial PFOS concentration of 15.26 μM, the residual PFOS concentration after the first adsorption cycle is 2.62 μM, indicating that Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY had reached its adsorption capacity. Based on this result, the adsorption capacity of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY for PFOS was determined to be 0.126 mg/g. The overall performance of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY is compared with that of several representative literature systems for the detection or adsorption of PFOS, as shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information). It is observed that the LOD value of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY is lower than or comparable to literature values. Most of these literature sensing systems, however, have no adsorption or removal for the analyte (PFOS). Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY is superior to these literature systems by satisfying sensing and adsorption simultaneously. The unsatisfactory adsorption capacity of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY, compared to that of MC-PB (69 mg/g), is explained by the EY:CTAB-filled pores in Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY, leaving limited space for PFOS adsorption.

In this study, we developed a core–shell composite structure (Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY) for the sensing, adsorption, and removal of PFOS. CTAB functioned as an auxiliary reagent for Eosin Y, inducing an emission turn-on effect of Eosin Y:CTAB upon interaction with PFOS. The optimal molar ratio of [EY]:[CTAB] was determined to be 0.5 μM:80 μM. Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY was thoroughly characterized using SEM, magnetism analysis, XRD, N2 adsorption/desorption, and TGA (thermal gravimetric analysis). The probe loading level was determined to be 28.5 % based on TGA results. The emission turn-on effect was attributed to the viscosity changes in the micelles caused by PFOS adsorption. The sensing behavior followed a linear Stern-Volmer relationship, described by the equation I/I0 = 1.081 + 0.144 × 106[PFOS], R2 = 0.990. The sensor exhibited high selectivity toward PFOS, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.27 μ M. Additionally, Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY demonstrated efficient PFOS removal, achieving an adsorption capacity of 0.126 mg/g. For future work, efforts should focus on enhancing probe sensitivity and increasing probe loading levels to further improve both the optical sensing performance and adsorption efficiency.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The Supporting Information contains: the performance comparison between Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY and literature systems; TEM images of Fe3O4@SiO2 and Fe3O4@MCM-41, an overall SEM view and the elemental mapping of Fe3O4@MCM-41/EY; the geometric parameters of EY in three configurations (simulated ones and experimentally obtained ones). Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126356.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
